title: Quantum speed limit for measurement probabilities
abstract: Any protocol to process quantum information has to conclude with a measurement, aimed at producing a specific set of probabilities of measurement outcomes. In this work, we investigate the time, energy and importantly the genuine quantum resources necessary for transforming a set of measurement probabilities generated by a positive-operator-valued measure (POVM), to a target set of measurement probabilities. To this end, we first show that the speed of measurement probabilities, defined as the average rate of the surprisal of measurement outcomes, is constrained by the genuine quantum fluctuations contained in the measurement probabilities. Interestingly, this quantum speed limit can act as a witness for bipartite quantum correlations by selecting an optimal local projective measurement. Furthermore, we obtain a minimum time to transform an initial measurement probabilities to a target measurement probabilities, and apply this result to analyzing the cost of generating a local athermality in terms of genuine quantum uncertainty.
summary: This paper establishes a new type of quantum speed limit specifically for the evolution of measurement probabilities. It shows that the speed at which these probabilities change is fundamentally constrained by the amount of quantumness (noncommutativity) and energy available in the system. The authors demonstrate that this limit can be used to detect quantum correlations and to calculate the minimum time required to drive a system away from thermal equilibrium. This provides a deeper understanding of the physical resources required for quantum information tasks.
main_problem: The paper investigates the fundamental limits on the speed at which measurement probabilities can evolve, specifically determining the minimum time, energy, and quantum resources required to transform one set of probabilities into another.
main_result: The authors derive a quantum speed limit (QSL) where the speed of measurement probabilities is upper-bounded by the product of the energetic cost and a quantumness resource (noncommutativity). They also show this limit can witness bipartite correlations and quantify the time needed to generate local athermality.
method: The study utilizes mathematical tools such as Jensen's inequality, Hölder inequality, and Schatten p-norms, alongside the Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability framework to bound the rate of change of surprisal.
model_or_system: The work considers a quantum system with a finite-dimensional Hilbert space undergoing continuous-time unitary evolution generated by a Hamiltonian H(t), including bipartite systems (system and ancilla) and single-qubit models.
key_observables: Measurement probabilities (POVM outcomes), surprisal, quantumness resource (U_M), local athermality, and Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability.
important_parameters: Energetic cost (Hamiltonian norm), quantumness resource (noncommutativity), and the time-average of measurement uncertainty.
main_assumptions: The primary derivation assumes unitary dynamics and a bounded Hamiltonian to avoid infinite speeds.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of probability evolution speed, defines the mathematical framework for the speed of measurement probabilities, derives the fundamental quantum speed limit, demonstrates applications to bipartite correlation witnessing and athermality generation, and provides supplementary proofs for qubit complementarity.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum information and open quantum systems as it provides a new fundamental bound on information processing rates based on quantumness and uncertainty rather than just state-space distance.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
